response:
**Decision recorded: ADR 0045** (`docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-forcing-function.md`, status `accepted`).

## Decision
Adopt the **sync-before-spend rule (O1)**, but implemented as a script-invoked forcing function rather than bare CLAUDE.md prose — mirroring how this repo already treats `adr-lint`/`/retrospect` (a run-this step, not a remember-this fact):
1. A new read-only script (e.g. `scripts/preflight-issue.mjs`) that `git fetch`s and greps merged/open PRs and commits for the target issue number, WARNing if it's already closed elsewhere.
2. One CLAUDE.md Shipping-section line naming this script mandatory before executing a dispatched issue and again before final push.

**Reject O2 and O3** (the claim protocol, alone or combined). **Reject O4** (no mechanism).

## Why
The advisor table's raw average favored O3 (3, 4, 4), but all three advisors converged on the same disqualifying fact for O2/O3: ISSUE-106.md:15 states issue-write permission is *sometimes withheld* by dispatch instructions — not hypothetical, stated. A claim signal that can be silently absent for a reason unrelated to actual safety (no permission vs. genuinely unclaimed) is worse than no signal at all: a second session sees "no claim" and can't tell those two cases apart, manufacturing false confidence rather than preventing the error — an anti-poka-yoke. Stacking that onto O1 (O3) piles process weight onto a single incident the issue itself flags as luck, not design — exactly what CLAUDE.md's don't-gold-plate rule warns against.

That leaves O1 as the right target — the root cause was a stale read at spend time, which only re-sync catches. The one legitimate critique of O1 (all three advisors raised it) was that *bare prose* has the same enforcement weakness that just failed once. This repo's own established answer to that — used for adr-lint and `/retrospect` — is to pair the rule with a runnable script, not trust memory. That's the refinement I made to O1 rather than adopting it as pure prose.

O4 is dismissed on the issue's own terms ("that was luck, not design," ISSUE-106.md:6), consistent with ADR 0042's precedent of mechanizing pre-spend discipline after one costly recurrence rather than tolerating it.

## Falsifiable criteria / revisit triggers
- `[checkable]`: the preflight script ships read-only with a unit test of its match/no-match logic.
- `[unverifiable]` + REOPEN-IF: if a dispatched session skips running the script before spend even once post-ship, that argues for wiring the check into dispatch tooling itself rather than relying on a session-run script.
- Separately: if dispatch instructions come to reliably grant issue-write permission on every dispatch, O2/O3's disqualifying flaw disappears and they should be reopened.

The ADR sits at ~5,370 chars (well under the 6,000 budget) and cites only ADR 0042 (on disk), so it should clear `adr-lint` — I wasn't able to execute the lint script or PDCA advisor tooling in this sandbox (Bash node/python invocations were denied), so I hand-verified its checks (frontmatter, id/filename match, no dangling `ADR NNNN` cites, no release-version strings, a `[checkable]` criterion present, char budget) against the lint source directly. Worth running `node pdca-workflow/scripts/adr-lint.mjs docs/decisions` yourself before this merges.

Implementation (the script, the CLAUDE.md line, and the separate foreground-wait lesson from ISSUE-106.md:18) is deferred to follow-up issues per CLAUDE.md's Deferred rule — this turn only produces the decision record.

--- recorded decision file docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-forcing-function.md ---

id: 0045
title: "Sync-before-spend as a forcing function, not a claim protocol"
status: accepted
summary: "#106 (issue #30 executed twice, ~$5-8 lost): adopt sync-before-spend as a script-invoked forcing function (mirroring adr-lint/retrospect), not bare CLAUDE.md prose; reject the claim protocol (O2/O3) since its issue-write permission is stated as sometimes withheld, making it a silently-absent, not reliable, signal."
---

# 0045 — Sync-before-spend as a forcing function, not a claim protocol

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: lean-process-engineer, process-economist, session-operator (per ISSUE-106.md's 3-advisor, 2-round estimate)
- Context: ISSUE-106.md:6 — a dispatched session ran issue #30 in full (~$5-8) while main already carried the merged fix (PR #97); the issue read `open` at dispatch but was stale-by-use by execution. ISSUE-106.md:10 names the gap as no forced re-sync and no visible claim. O3 scored highest on raw average (3,4,4) but all three advisors flagged the same weak point on O2/O3: the claim protocol needs issue-write permission that "dispatch instructions sometimes withhold" (ISSUE-106.md:15) — a stated, not hypothetical, constraint.

## Decision
Adopt the **sync-before-spend rule**, implemented as a forcing function, not prose alone:
1. New deterministic script (e.g. `scripts/preflight-issue.mjs`): `git fetch origin main`, then grep merged/open PRs and commit subjects for the target issue number, WARN if it looks already closed elsewhere. Read-only, same design-constraint class as `adr-lint.mjs`.
2. CLAUDE.md's Shipping section gains one line naming this script mandatory before executing a dispatched issue and again before final push — the pattern already used for `/retrospect` and `adr-lint` (a run-this step, not a remember-this fact).
Do not adopt the claim protocol (O2); therefore not O3 (both).

## Justification
Root cause was a stale read at spend time (TOCTOU) — only re-sync catches that; the claim protocol targets a different failure mode (long-running collisions) that didn't occur here. All three advisors' actual complaint about O1 was enforcement-under-pressure for *bare prose*, not its targeting — this repo's own answer to that is pairing prose with a script (poka-yoke), exactly as done for adr-lint/retrospect. That converts O1 from "the discipline that just failed once" into a named, invokable step — cheap and root-cause-targeted.
O2/O3 fail on a sharper ground than added friction: ISSUE-106.md:15 states issue-write permission is *sometimes withheld*, not merely untested. A claim signal that can be silently absent for a reason unrelated to safety (no permission, vs. actually unclaimed) is worse than no signal — a second session finding no claim can't tell "no one's on this" from "the first session couldn't post." That's anti-poka-yoke: manufactured false confidence, not error prevention. Stacking it onto O1 (O3) is muda against one incident ISSUE-106.md itself calls luck, not design (line 6) — CLAUDE.md's don't-gold-plate rule applies directly. O4 is rejected outright: the issue text rejects the luck framing on its own terms, and this repo's adjacent precedent (ADR 0042) mechanizes pre-spend discipline after one costly recurrence, not tolerates it.

## Assumptions
- [verified] Incident and gap as stated in ISSUE-106.md:6,10 — read 2026-07-14.
- [checkable] `scripts/preflight-issue.mjs` exists, is read-only (no push/issue-write calls), and has a unit test of its match/no-match logic per CLAUDE.md's "no process-gating script without a test of its decision logic" — owner: implementer, at the PR adding it.
- [checkable] CLAUDE.md's Shipping section names the script as mandatory pre-execute and pre-final-push — owner: implementer/reviewer on that PR's diff (adr-lint doesn't enforce CLAUDE.md prose content).
- [unverifiable] A script-invoked forcing function is followed materially more reliably under spend pressure than bare prose was — REOPEN-IF a dispatched session skips running it before spend at least once post-ship (ADR 0042's "recurs once more → escalate" precedent), arguing for wiring the check into dispatch tooling itself instead.

## Rejected alternatives
- O2 (claim alone) — targets long-running collisions, not the observed failure; its issue-write permission is stated as sometimes unavailable, so the signal is unreliable.
- O3 (both) — inherits O2's silent-absence flaw; stacking claim machinery onto one self-corrected, luck-flagged incident is muda.
- O4 (no mechanism) — rejected by ISSUE-106.md's own "luck, not design" framing; conflicts with ADR 0042's mechanize-after-one-recurrence precedent.
- O1 as bare prose (no script) — same enforcement weakness ISSUE-106.md names (line 14); this repo prefers a script forcing function over trusting memory of a rule.

## Revisit triggers
- Preflight script skipped at least once post-ship → escalate to dispatch-tooling-level wiring, per the REOPEN-IF above.
- Dispatch instructions come to reliably grant issue-write permission on every dispatch (removing line 15's constraint) → reopen O2/O3.
- A long-running (not stale-read) collision occurs despite this ADR → re-evaluate a claim-style signal for that distinct failure mode.

Related: subagent dispatch prompts should mandate foreground waits on long child runs (ISSUE-106.md:18) — track as a follow-up issue, not part of this decision.
